# Transcrição: Building Your Service Document

- **Fonte:** Building Your Service Document

## [00:00 - 03:08]

Okay, so I'm going to show you how to set up your service document, which is your pricing document or a pricing menu, I should say. I've started to flush this one out a little bit. So I'm going to talk to you about what I've done and then what we're going to do. We're going to be essentially calculating how much a service offer is going to be costing us internally. And then we're also going to be calculating how much we should be selling that service for through this service document. Now, remember you have this document in place so that it protects both yourself as well as the client. You can obviously find fluidity in how much you decide to ultimately charge clients at the end of the day, but you have to realize that the margins need to shift as a result. So what I'm meaning by that is if you decide to sell whatever service you're selling in your service document for less than what your service document stipulates or what you end up calculating, just make sure that those margins that that's going to be aiding into are also looked at or addressed because that's going to need to come from somewhere else in your business. Okay, so the first thing that we need to do for this is to scope out who is required to deliver the service so that we can then calculate the costs or the production costs of that service. So to get to that, I actually need to list out exactly what's included in the specific service. So I've started out by listing on the left hand column here under service the services that we offer. ## [03:08 - 06:10]

these days for some reason. But let's say that you're building websites for real estate agents and you go, okay, well the first step is to actually get the branding or the wire framing done. I don't offer the service so I'm making this stuff up. But you basically list out what is the process behind that service. So for us, the first process is messaging and then it's the strategy. So our account managers pick out the best strategy for the client based on where, you know, the resources they have access to or where they're at and on and on it goes. And I try to flush that out as much as possible and you can see here I've even added asterix and made some notes in there of what it includes and doesn't include. And the more detailed you can get in this section, guys, the better it's going to be for you. So really this entire exercise can take you quite a few hours. It can take you a day and that's okay. That's really normal but it's so important and this piece of this document in your business is going to create so much automation for you. It's not even funny. So it's going to allow you to step away from your business. So it's a really important document. The other part of this is why getting flushed out is really, really important here is this is the stuff that you put in your contract. So when you bring a new client on board under your terms of service, you should be listing out the deliverables because you never want to get into a situation where a client is saying, well, you promised me this and this and this and it's a he said, she said situation. You want to be really clear of like this is what you're paying me for. You're not paying me for your business to make $100 million or whatever. So to protect yourself and you list this out under a deliverable section of your contract or your agreement and it clearly outlines okay, while you had two revisions on your email sequences and you requested three and this it was stipulated in the contract that you know this would be additional charge and we would warn you about this and and it protects you as an agency. So this is why this document is really important because not only is it going to give you so much clarity in terms of the positioning of your business, right? Because your price point plays a big part in how you position yourself. And if you're going to be an agency selling $50,000 services, the way you position yourself perhaps needs to be different than someone who's selling you know $2,000 services.

So this document is your business in many ways. It's the it's the heart of your business. So it's really important that we spend a lot of time on this. Who am I going to need to build out the funnel wireframes and the integrations and then speed checking and all of that stuff. So this is setting up a funnel. Is this one person's job or is there a couple people required behind this? This is basically what you're now trying to assess. If you don't know what this is, then you're probably trying to sell a service that you don't have a competence in. So again, highly recommend stick to the stuff that you're really competent with even if you're thinking, God, I don't want to be doing this. This is the whole point. ## [09:19 - 12:22]

So this is a different person and then this is a different person. Now if you are, what we're trying to do now is we're going to try to calculate, well, how much do these people cost us inside of the agency? So assess the cost of those human resources. If you're using contractors, we're going to estimate the internal cost of the project by multiplying the hours required by the cost of the contractor. So once you identify the processes that are part of this service, you then want to map out how long those processes take. So for example, messaging, it could go, well, this will take about an hour to do properly. In fact, it's probably at two hours actually. Funnel strategy, that's about an hour because that's actually really processed properly. So it doesn't require someone to look into a crystal ball for three weeks to figure that out. But essentially what you're doing is you're writing out how much time. Now if you don't know how much time this takes. Again, it's either going to be for one or two reasons. The first one is that you've never done it before or the second reason you have done this service before, but you never actually really tracked. And if you are in the latter camp where you've never officially tracked how much time something has taken you, then I highly recommend you take part in that exercise. You're going to have to build this stuff anyways, right? Because you're going to have to record yourself going through messaging, funnel strategies, etc., etc. So that you can process it and so that you can train people to replace you to do that. So if you have never actually taken proper measures to calculate how much time something has taken you, then I highly recommend that you do that. ## [12:22 - 15:23]

people internally in your business, then you're going to be tapping into contractors for a big part of this and or you're probably going to be doing some of this until you start to get that momentum going with your sales and your leads pipeline. But let's say you're using contractors, what we then do is we go, okay, eight hours by the cost of our contractors. Now typically speaking, contractors are normally going to cost you between 50 to $180 depending on the skill and experience required. Just to give you a bit of context, like eight loop we pay $60 an hour for our contractors and if you decide to build a production team outside of the US or outside of your local area, then for sure you can start to get into much lower prices, like you can find some really skilled people for $20 an hour. But that comes with its own challenges as well, so just something to be aware of. So when you multiply this, so let's say we're going with the contracting cost $60 an hour and your job here guys is to go and find your production team. This is exactly what you have to do, right? If you're not going to find a production team, who's going to start replacing you from doing this stuff and even if it's contractors and even if you don't have a client yet, you should have your production team ready to go. You should be assessing them and at the very least understanding how much it's going to cost you once you're ready to go because otherwise how do you know how much to even sell it for, right? It doesn't make sense to get on calls with clients when you're pitching them something that you don't actually know what it's going to cost you to deliver. So let's say we go off $60 an hour here. I'm really bad at math, so I'm just going to quickly pull up my calculator and do 8 times $60 because 8 hours is the time. So this, my internal cost is $480. Now in order to calculate how much I should sell this for for the client, I'm essentially going to divide this by my cost per goods margin. So what I mean by this is remember when we talked about your margins in your business, I gave you a template where our costs of goods was about 25% of the revenue essentially. So $480, this internal cost represents 25% of that revenue.

## [15:23 - 18:27]

So what I do is I go 480 divided by 0.25 and I get a cost of 19.20. So this is how much I should be selling this service for to the client in order to have the margins that I want. Now the other thing is the other way to calculate this is if you have an internal team, you will essentially have your internal hourly rate that you would have calculated. Whatever that might be, usually it's between 170 to, well, we're at 290. So for example, our internal hourly rate, 297. So again, this isn't what we show clients, but this is what we know we need to maintain within the agency in order to keep the margins that we're trying to keep to. So that's $297. So down here, our cost would actually be $2,376 for this service. So you can start to see just how much your production team costs you and the margins that you're trying to work with will impact the price points significantly. And that in itself can change your positioning in the market. This isn't a big difference, but when you're only considering this as eight hours, this is miscalculated. This is probably more like a 10, 13 hour job all up. But when you're looking at your production team and the costs of your business in terms of how much does it cost you internally and you know these numbers ahead of time, you start to really protect yourself as a company. And this is when you get in situations where you actually can deliver great service that can replace you. So you're hiring people that are just as good as you are. You've processed it in the way that you like things done. And now you're not skimping or you're not trying to just undercut or under scope yourself everywhere you go. You fully understand how much time it should take and what that time costs you so that you can operate as a business. So this is basically how to put together your service document. A couple of other things I just want to mention here. If you wanted to calculate a discounted price, so let's say you were telling your sales team, well when you're getting on calls with leads and you feel like they're really, really good for you, for us or the vision of where we're going and you can see huge opportunities with them, it's okay to give them a discount.

## [18:27 - 19:58]

What you need to consider now is go, okay, well that discount is going to come out of somewhere else, right? So normally what most businesses would do is you waive your profit margin. So if your profit margin is 5%, then you can give a 5% discount without it costing you. Beyond that, it starts to cost you as a business. So again, this is an important number to know, right? These costs because now you know exactly where you start to bite into your own business and onboarding a client because a future potential starts to actually financially cost you or it's something that you can waive. So that's how you would normally calculate that is you would just take out your profit margin which means you broke even on that client which is, you know, if you see potential there that's more than worth it. If you see a lot of potential, you may even decide to discount it further, but understanding these numbers here means you know exactly how much that will cost you internally. Now if you're trying to price things on performance base, go under the pricing training because I show you how to calculate that model. But this is how to set up your service document and why this document is like the, you know, the heart of everything that you're going to be doing as an agency in terms of your marketing, how you put yourself out there and the types of people that you attract into your business.
